title: Weekly Market Report | Jun 22-26, 2026
report:
# Weekly Market Report | June 22-26, 2026

---

## Japan Market Overview

The Nikkei posted its first sharp weekly decline in two weeks. After the prior week's record surge of over 5,000 points, this week became a "memory frenzy" rollercoaster, with memory and AI semiconductor stocks whipsawing the entire index. Intraday ranges reached 2,500-3,200 points on three separate days -- a clear spike in volatility at all-time-high territory.

**Nikkei 225 Weekly Path**
- Mon 6/22: 72,353 (+1,103). Eighth straight gain, fresh all-time high. Intraday YTD high of 72,831
- Tue 6/23: 69,788 (-2,565 / -4.0%). Fifth-largest point drop on record; back below 70,000 for the first time in four sessions. Index P/E stretched to the upper bound; SoftBank Group plunged and Kioxia briefly broke below 100,000 yen
- Wed 6/24: 69,174 (-613). Continued decline but showed resilience
- Thu 6/25: 72,366 (+3,191). Fourth-largest point gain in history. Micron's blowout earnings (revenue x4.5, net income x15) validated AI memory demand and drove a new record closing high
- Fri 6/26: 69,360 (-3,005). Back below 70,000 in a single day. SoftBank Group fell -12.5% (reports of OpenAI IPO delay), Kioxia -11.2%. Sharp losses in Korean equities and reports of a halted Strait of Hormuz escape route added pressure

**Weekly Change: 71,250 -> 69,360, -1,889 (-2.65%)**

Foreign investors turned net buyers (1.06 trillion yen) in the third week of June for the first time in four weeks -- their behavior on dips is next week's key focus.

---

## Big Events This Week

**Micron Reignites the AI Memory Trade (6/24 after close)**

Revenue up 4.5x year-on-year and net income up 15x for the Mar-May quarter. Soaring HBM/DRAM prices flowed straight to the bottom line, propelling Tokyo to a historic rally on 6/25. At the same time, cost concerns surfaced for memory *buyers*, exposing the trade's double-edged nature.

**SoftBank Group Plunge and Kioxia Whiplash**

Reports of an OpenAI IPO delay sent SoftBank Group down -12.5% on 6/26. Kioxia, Japan's largest company by market cap, swung more than +/-10% repeatedly through the week, dragging the index with it.

---

## Weekly Theme Ranking & Analysis

### Top 5 Gainers
Optical Communication led the week, with Fujikura spiking as much as +14%. Beneath the AI turbulence, money rotated defensively into Defense & Aerospace, Banking, and High Dividend names.

### Bottom 5 Decliners
This year's leading themes -- Memory, AI Semiconductor, Semiconductor Equipment -- all finished the week lower. Profit-taking spread to electronic components as analysts cut ratings on Taiyo Yuden.

---

## U.S. Market

The Dow stayed firm on Micron's results and the Fed's bank stress test (all banks passed), while the Nasdaq lagged as markets priced in "memory inflation = Big Tech cost pressure." The Magnificent 7 underperformance highlights a qualitative shift in the AI trade on both sides of the Pacific.

---

## Key Economic Data

- May department store sales: +8.3% YoY (5th straight monthly gain, led by art/jewelry/precious metals)
- Oracle Japan FY5/26: net income +4.6% to 63.5 billion yen (cloud & datacenter strength)
- Foreign investor flows (3rd week of June): net buying of 1.06 trillion yen (first in 4 weeks)

---

## What to Watch Next Week (6/29-7/3)

1. **BOJ Tankan (7/1)**: Consensus for the large manufacturers' DI is +16. Watch for AI capex breadth
2. **U.S. Jobs Report (7/2)**: Released Thursday due to Independence Day; will steer Fed rate-hike expectations
3. **Kioxia & SoftBank Group price action**: Stabilization in these two heavyweights is a precondition for an index rebound
4. **Post-dividend reinvestment flows**: With June-end dividend payments done, supply-demand momentum may shift

---

## StockWaveJP Editorial View

Intraday swings of 3,000 points argue for keeping leverage firmly in check. We see the long-term AI boom as intact, but a pullback toward the 6-week moving average (low-67,000s) is within normal range. This was a week that rewarded portfolios holding pillars beyond AI -- optical communication, defense, and banks -- rather than chasing the index.
